title: Practice Problems: Final Test Study Guide

cards.definition: A controlled market experiment assigning participants randomly to a control version (A) and treatment version (B) to compare outcome metrics and establish causal impact of a product, feature, price, or message change.
A name, term, sign, symbol, design, or combination thereof intended to identify the goods or services of one seller and differentiate them from competitors.
The added value a brand name confers on a product beyond its functional benefits, reflected in consumer perceptions, preferences, and behaviors.

The highest level of Keller's Brand Resonance Pyramid: a deep psychological relationship between consumer and brand expressed through behavioral loyalty, attitudinal attachment, sense of community, and active engagement.

A quantitative concept selection tool that multiplies consumer-derived importance weights by concept ratings on each criterion and sums the products for a total score per concept; used after screening to distinguish among surviving concepts.
A coarse concept selection tool using a +/0/− scale relative to a reference concept to eliminate clearly inferior alternatives before applying the more detailed scoring matrix.

A quantitative concept testing technique that decomposes overall consumer preference into part-worth contributions of each product attribute and level via regression; used to identify important attributes and simulate preference for new configurations.
The sequence of activities from project start to finish that takes the longest time to complete, determining minimum total project duration; all activities on the critical path have zero slack.

An experimental design that tests all combinations of multiple independent variables simultaneously, enabling estimation of main effects and interaction effects; more efficient than running separate A/B tests for each factor.

A customer research philosophy framing needs as functional, social, or emotional jobs consumers are trying to accomplish, revealing latent needs and non-obvious competitors more effectively than attribute-focused surveys.
A four-level brand building model: Brand Salience → Brand Performance + Brand Imagery → Brand Judgments + Brand Feelings → Brand Resonance; each level must be built before the next can be achieved.
A new product that is an addition to an existing product line of the firm, designed to broaden the portfolio for the firm's current markets.

A product architecture composed of standardized, interchangeable modules combined in different configurations to produce variety without redesigning from scratch; enables mass customization and reduces inventory risk under demand uncertainty.

A graphical representation of project activities and precedence relationships used to identify the critical path, compute slack times, and make crashing decisions via forward pass and backward pass calculations.

The contribution of a specific attribute level to a consumer's overall product preference, estimated as a regression coefficient in conjoint analysis; used to calculate attribute importance and simulate preference for new product profiles.

A framework for evaluating industry competitive attractiveness through five structural forces: Threat of New Entrants, Bargaining Power of Buyers, Bargaining Power of Suppliers, Threat of Substitutes, and Intensity of Rivalry.

The deliberate reduction of activity durations by adding resources to shorten total project duration at additional cost; prioritizes the critical path activity with the lowest crash cost per unit of time saved.

A three-question opportunity evaluation framework: Is it Real? (genuine need + technical achievability), Can We Win? (sustainable competitive advantage), Is it Worth It? (sufficient financial return at acceptable risk); all three must be satisfied.
The amount of time a project activity can be delayed without delaying total project completion; computed as LS − ES or LF − EF; activities on the critical path have slack = 0.

Three nested market sizing estimates: Total Addressable Market (100% share), Serviceable Addressable Market (realistic target segment), and Serviceable Obtainable Market (credibly capturable share given competition); SOM is the operationally relevant figure.

A hierarchical decomposition of a project into progressively smaller components and individual work packages, serving as the input for constructing a project network diagram and identifying precedence relationships.
